content:
# Acts Chapter 17 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 246-247 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_017_page_001.jpg, ACT_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having-traveled-through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they-came to Thessalonica, where there-was a synagogue of-the Jews.

**2** And according-to the [thing] having-been-customary for-Paul, he-went-in to them, and for three sabbaths he-reasoned with-them from the Scriptures,

**3** opening and setting-forth that the Christ had to-suffer and to-rise from [the] dead, and that "This is the Christ, Jesus, whom I am-proclaiming to-you."

**4** And some of them were-persuaded and were-allotted to-Paul and Silas, and of-the God-fearing Greeks a great multitude, and of-the prominent women not a few.

**5** But the Jews, having-become-jealous, and having-taken-along certain wicked men of-the marketplace-loungers, and having-formed-a-mob, were-throwing-into-uproar the city, and having-set-upon the house of-Jason, they-were-seeking them to-bring-forth to the populace.

**6** And not having-found them, they-were-dragging Jason and certain brothers before the city-rulers, crying-out that "The-[ones] having-upset the inhabited-[world], these also have-come here,

**7** whom Jason has-received; and these all are-acting contrary-to the decrees of-Caesar, saying a different [one] to-be king, Jesus."

**8** And they-troubled the crowd and the city-rulers hearing these-[things].

**9** And having-taken the security-bond from Jason and the rest, they-released them.

**10** And the brothers immediately through the night sent-out both Paul and Silas to Berea, who having-arrived went into the synagogue of-the Jews.

**11** And these were more-noble than the-[ones] in Thessalonica, who received the word with all eagerness, daily examining the Scriptures [to see] if these-[things] might-be so.

**12** Indeed therefore many of them believed, and of-the prominent Greek women and men not a few.

**13** But when the Jews from Thessalonica knew that also in Berea the word of-God was-proclaimed by Paul, they-came also there, stirring-up and troubling the crowds.

**14** And then immediately the brothers sent-out Paul to-go as-far-as the sea; but both Silas and Timothy remained there.

**15** And the-[ones] conducting Paul brought [him] as-far-as Athens, and having-received a command to Silas and Timothy that as quickly-as-possible they-should-come to him, they-departed.

**16** And in Athens, while Paul was-waiting-for them, his spirit was-being-provoked in him, seeing the city being full-of-idols.

**17** Indeed therefore he-was-reasoning in the synagogue with-the Jews and the God-fearing-[ones], and in the marketplace every day with the-[ones] happening-to-be-present.

**18** And also certain of-the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers were-encountering him, and some were-saying, "What might this seed-picker wish to-say?" And others, "He-seems to-be a proclaimer of-foreign divinities" — because he-was-proclaiming-the-good-news [of] Jesus and the resurrection.

**19** And having-taken-hold-of him, to the Areopagus they-brought [him], saying, "Are-we-able to-know what [is] this new teaching being-spoken by you?

**20** For you-are-bringing certain strange-[things] to our ears; we-wish therefore to-know what these-[things] might-wish to-be."

**21** Now all [the] Athenians and the foreigners residing-[there] were-spending-time for nothing other than to-say or to-hear something newer.

**22** And Paul, having-stood in [the] midst of-the Areopagus, said, "Men, Athenians, in all-[things] I-perceive you as being more-religious-than-others.

**23** For passing-through and observing your objects-of-worship, I-found also an altar on which had-been-inscribed, 'To-an-Unknown God.' What therefore [you] being-ignorant worship, this I am-proclaiming to-you.

**24** The God, the-[One] having-made the world and all the-[things] in it, this-[One] being Lord of-heaven and of-earth, does not dwell in hand-made temples,

**25** nor by hands of-men is-He-served, as needing anything, He-Himself giving to-all life and breath and all-[things];

**26** and He-made from one every nation of-men to-dwell upon all [the] face of-the earth, having-determined appointed seasons and the boundaries of-the dwelling of-them,

**27** to-seek God, if indeed they-might-grope-for Him and find [Him], and indeed not being far from each one of-us He-exists.

**28** For in Him we-live and we-move and we-are, as also certain of-the poets among you have-said, 'For of-Him also we-are offspring.'

**29** Therefore being offspring of-God, we-ought not to-think [that] the Divine is-like gold or silver or stone, [an] engraving of-art and imagination of-man.

**30** Indeed therefore the times of-ignorance having-overlooked, God now is-commanding men, all everywhere, to-repent,

**31** because He-set a day in which He-is-about to-judge the inhabited-[world] in righteousness, by a man whom He-appointed, having-offered proof to-all, having-raised him from [the] dead."

**32** And having-heard [of the] resurrection of-[the] dead, some indeed were-mocking, but others said, "We-will-hear you concerning this also again."

**33** Thus Paul went-out from [the] midst of-them.

**34** But certain men, having-joined-themselves to-him, believed, among whom [were] also Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman by-name Damaris and others with them.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Acts 17 — Codex Sinaiticus Observations**

1. **v. 4 — "were-allotted" (proseklerothesan):** Sinaiticus reads proseklerothesan (were-allotted/assigned), a passive form meaning "were-joined-by-lot." This is the distinctive Sinaiticus reading. The Byzantine tradition reads prosethesan ("were-added"), a simpler construction. The Sinaiticus reading preserves the more vivid and lectio difficilior form.

2. **v. 5 — "the Jews":** Sinaiticus includes hoi Ioudaioi as the subject of the jealousy. Some manuscripts add hoi apeithountes ("the disobedient/unbelieving [ones]") here; Sinaiticus omits this qualifier, reading the simpler text.

3. **v. 6 — "the inhabited-[world]" (ten oikoumenen):** The famous phrase — "these who have upset the inhabited-world" (hoi ten oikoumenen anastatosantes). Sinaiticus preserves this reading clearly.

4. **v. 13 — Sinaiticus reads "stirring up and troubling the crowds":** Some later manuscripts omit "and troubling" (kai tarassontes). Sinaiticus preserves the fuller reading with both participles, saleuontes kai tarassontes.

5. **v. 14 — "as far as the sea" (heos epi ten thalassan):** Sinaiticus reads heos epi ten thalassan. This is debated — some take it as Paul actually traveling by sea to Athens, others as a diversionary tactic. The Western text (Codex D) reads differently, suggesting Paul went past Thessaly. I translate what Sinaiticus plainly states.

6. **v. 18 — "seed-picker" (spermologos):** A distinctly Athenian slang term, literally "one who picks up seeds" like a bird in the marketplace — i.e., a scavenger of secondhand ideas. I preserve the literal compound rather than smoothing to "babbler."

7. **v. 26 — "from one" (ex henos):** Sinaiticus reads ex henos without the noun haimatos ("blood"). The Byzantine text adds "one blood." The Sinaiticus reading leaves the phrase compressed: "from one" — whether "one [man]" or "one [origin]" is grammatically ambiguous. I preserve the manuscript's spare reading.

8. **v. 30 — "having-overlooked" (hyperidoon):** Sinaiticus uses the aorist participle hyperidoon. This is God's sovereign looking-past of former ignorance — not "winking at" (KJV) but a deliberate overlooking, now terminated by the command to repent.

9. **v. 34 — "Dionysius the Areopagite":** Sinaiticus clearly preserves both names — Dionysius and Damaris. Later tradition identified Dionysius as the first bishop of Athens. The text itself says only that he was a member of the Areopagus council.

10. **General note on scribal hand:** The text on pages 246-247 is written in the primary hand (scribe A) of Sinaiticus, with the characteristic continuous uncial script (scriptio continua) and no word divisions. No significant corrector interventions are visible in this chapter.

— **EMET**
